title: The short forward draw
subtitle: The standard drafting method for consistent, smooth yarn on the spinning wheel
excerpt: The short forward draw is the most controlled drafting technique for wheel spinning. One hand pinches to regulate twist while the other drafts forward. The result is a smooth, consistent yarn suitable for all plied yarn weights. This tutorial covers hand position, drafting rhythm, and tension settings.
sourceNotes: The short forward draw (sometimes called the inch-worm draw or supported draw) is standard wheel-spinning technique described consistently in handspinning instruction. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: The hole at the front of the spinning wheel flyer through which spun yarn travels from the drafting zone onto the bobbin. Yarn tension and twist are regulated at the orifice end.
The spool on the spinning wheel that collects spun yarn. Bobbins are held on the flyer spindle and rotate relative to the flyer as yarn winds on.
A single-drive wheel system where a brake band on the bobbin controls uptake. The drive band runs over the flyer whorl only. Loosening the brake band reduces uptake draw-in.
The zone between the supply hand and the twist front where fibres are being thinned. The shape of this zone determines how much control you have over the yarn diameter.

content.text: In the short forward draw, the back hand holds the fibre supply and controls how much enters the
drafting triangle
, while the front hand pinches between the twist front and the
orifice
to stop twist entering the undrafted fibre. The back hand drafts forward in short increments, then the front hand slides back and repinches. Yarn winds onto the
bobbin
on each forward movement.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing clear of the drive band and flyer. Switch the wheel off before threading or adjusting tension.

content.text: Setting up
Thread a leader yarn through the
orifice
and tie it onto the bobbin. Join your fibre supply to the leader end as you would on a spindle (thin the roving end and overlap it with the leader). Set the
scotch tension
so the wheel draws yarn in firmly but not so aggressively that it pulls the fibre from your hands before you can draft.
Method
Step 1: Position both hands

Sit directly in front of the orifice. Hold the fibre supply in your back hand (furthest from the wheel), with the fibre running loosely through your fingers. Pinch firmly with your front hand just behind where twist is entering the yarn, 5-10 cm from the orifice.
Step 2: Draft forward

With the front hand pinching (stopping twist from entering the supply), slide the back hand forward toward the front hand, drafting a short length of fibre (3-5 cm). Open the front hand pinch briefly to allow twist into the drafted zone, then re-pinch with the front hand and slide the back hand forward again. Repeat: pinch front, draft back toward front, open front, re-pinch, draft again.
Step 3: Let the wheel draw on

As yarn forms, release tension slightly and let the wheel draw it onto the bobbin. The draw-in pull of the wheel should never fight your drafting hands: if the wheel is pulling too hard, loosen the brake band slightly. If yarn is not drawing in at all, tighten it.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Three problems in the short forward draw.

items.symptom: Twist runs into the fibre supply before drafting
items.cause: The front hand pinch is too loose or too far back from the orifice.
items.fix: Move the front hand closer to the orifice. Pinch more firmly. If twist runs into unspun fibre it forms a tangle that must be pulled out.

items.symptom: Yarn is thin in some places and thick in others
items.cause: Inconsistent draft length or opening the pinch before drafting is complete.
items.fix: Draft to a fixed length each time. Keep the front hand pinch closed until the back hand has finished moving.

items.symptom: Wheel is pulling yarn faster than you can draft
items.cause: Scotch tension brake is too tight.
items.fix: Loosen the brake band by half a turn at a time until you can draft comfortably without the wheel snatching the fibre.
